(অসমীয়া [[ɔˈxɔmija]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Assamese "Help:IPA/Assamese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:As-%E0%A6%85%E0%A6%B8%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%80%E0%A6%AF%E0%A6%BC%E0%A6%BE.oga "File:As-অসমীয়া.oga"))[[8]](#cite_note-9) is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_language "Indo-Aryan language") spoken mainly in the north-eastern Indian state of [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam"), where it is an official language. It has long served as a *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* in parts of [Northeast India](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India").[[9]](#cite_note-Nagamese-10)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-11)[[1]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-1) It has over 15 million [native speakers](/wiki/Native_speakers "Native speakers") and 8.3 million [second language speakers](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") according to *[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")*[[2]](#cite_note-e28|asm-2), although this claim is widely disputed.[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*]

**Assamese**[[a]](#cite_note-8) or **Asamiya** (অসমীয়া [[ɔˈxɔmija]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Assamese "Help:IPA/Assamese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:As-%E0%A6%85%E0%A6%B8%E0%A6%AE%E0%A7%80%E0%A6%AF%E0%A6%BC%E0%A6%BE.oga "File:As-অসমীয়া.oga"))[[8]](#cite_note-9) is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_language "Indo-Aryan language") spoken mainly in the north-eastern Indian state of [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam"), where it is an official language. It has long served as a *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* in parts of [Northeast India](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India").[[9]](#cite_note-Nagamese-10)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-11)[[1]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-1) It has over 15 million [native speakers](/wiki/Native_speakers "Native speakers") and 8.3 million [second language speakers](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") according to *[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")*[[2]](#cite_note-e28|asm-2), although this claim is widely disputed.[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*]

[Nefamese](/wiki/Nefamese "Nefamese"), an Assamese-based [pidgin](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin") in [Arunachal Pradesh](/wiki/Arunachal_Pradesh "Arunachal Pradesh"), was used as a lingua franca before being replaced by [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi_language "Hindi language"); and [Nagamese](/wiki/Nagamese_Creole "Nagamese Creole"), an Assamese-based [Creole language](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language"),[[11]](#cite_note-12) continues to be widely used in [Nagaland](/wiki/Nagaland "Nagaland"). The [Kamtapuri language](/wiki/Kamtapuri_language "Kamtapuri language") of [Rangpur Division](/wiki/Rangpur_Division "Rangpur Division") of Bangladesh and the [Cooch Behar](/wiki/Cooch_Behar_district "Cooch Behar district") and [Jalpaiguri](/wiki/Jalpaiguri_district "Jalpaiguri district") districts of India is linguistically closer to Assamese, though the speakers identify with the [Bengali culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Bengal "Culture of Bengal") and the literary language.[[12]](#cite_note-13) In the past, it was the court language of the [Ahom kingdom](/wiki/Ahom_kingdom "Ahom kingdom") from the 17th century.[[13]](#cite_note-ahom-court-language-14)

Along with other [Eastern Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Eastern_Indo-Aryan_languages "Eastern Indo-Aryan languages"), Assamese evolved at least before the 7th century CE[[14]](#cite_note-15) from the middle Indo-Aryan [Magadhi Prakrit](/wiki/Magadhi_Prakrit "Magadhi Prakrit").[[15]](#cite_note-kakati41p9-16) Its sister languages include [Angika](/wiki/Angika "Angika"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), [Bishnupriya Manipuri](/wiki/Bishnupriya_Manipuri_language "Bishnupriya Manipuri language"), [Chakma](/wiki/Chakma_language "Chakma language"), [Chittagonian](/wiki/Chittagonian_language "Chittagonian language"), [Hajong](/wiki/Hajong_language "Hajong language"), [Rajbangsi](/wiki/Kamtapuri_language "Kamtapuri language"), [Surjapuri](/wiki/Surjapuri_language "Surjapuri language"), [Maithili](/wiki/Maithili_language "Maithili language"), [Rohingya](/wiki/Rohingya_language "Rohingya language") and [Sylheti](/wiki/Sylheti_language "Sylheti language"). It is written in the [Assamese alphabet](/wiki/Assamese_alphabet "Assamese alphabet"), an [abugida](/wiki/Abugida "Abugida") system, from left to right, with many [typographic ligatures](/wiki/Typographic_ligature "Typographic ligature").

Assamese was designated as a [classical Indian language](/wiki/Classical_languages_of_India "Classical languages of India") by the [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") on 3 October 2024 on account of its antiquity and literary traditions.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/East_magadhan_proto-languages_-_en.svg/250px-East_magadhan_proto-languages_-_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:East_magadhan_proto-languages_-_en.svg)

The proto-languages of the eastern Magadhan languages. [Kamarupi Prakrit](/wiki/Kamarupi_Prakrit "Kamarupi Prakrit") corresponds to ?proto-Kamarupa here, a hitherto un-reconstructed proto-language. [proto-Kamata](/wiki/KRNB_lects#proto-Kamata "KRNB lects") began to innovate unique features in the period 1250–1550 CE.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Silver_rupee_of_Rudra_Simha.jpg/250px-Silver_rupee_of_Rudra_Simha.jpg)](/wiki/File:Silver_rupee_of_Rudra_Simha.jpg)

Silver coin issued during the reign of [Rudra Singha](/wiki/Rudra_Singha "Rudra Singha") in Sanskrit with Assamese letters

Assamese originated in [Old Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Old-Indo-Aryan "Old-Indo-Aryan") dialects, though the exact nature of its origin and growth is not clear yet.[[18]](#cite_note-19) It is generally believed that Assamese and the [Kamatapuri lects](/wiki/Kamatapuri_lects "Kamatapuri lects") derive from the [Kamarupi dialect](/wiki/Kamarupi_Prakrit "Kamarupi Prakrit") of Eastern [Magadhi Prakrit](/wiki/Magadhi_Prakrit "Magadhi Prakrit")[[15]](#cite_note-kakati41p9-16) though some authors contest a close connection of Assamese with Magadhi Prakrit.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) The Indo-Aryan, which appeared in the 4th–5th century in Assam,[[21]](#cite_note-4th5th-22) was probably spoken in the new settlements of [Kamarupa](/wiki/Kamarupa "Kamarupa")—in urban centers and along the [Brahmaputra River](/wiki/Brahmaputra_River "Brahmaputra River")—surrounded by Tibeto-Burman and Austroasiatic communities.[[22]](#cite_note-23) Kakati's (1941) assertion that Assamese has an [Austroasiatic](/wiki/Austroasiatic "Austroasiatic") substrate is generally assumed—which suggests that when the Indo-Aryan centers formed in the 4th–5th centuries CE, there were substantial Austroasiatic speakers that later accepted the Indo-Aryan [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular").[[21]](#cite_note-4th5th-22) Based on the 7th-century Chinese traveller [Xuanzang](/wiki/Xuanzang "Xuanzang")'s observations, [Chatterji (1926)](#CITEREFChatterji1926) suggests that the Indo-Aryan vernacular differentiated itself in Kamarupa before it did in Bengal,[[23]](#cite_note-24) and that these differences could be attributed to non-Indo-Aryan speakers adopting the language.[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoral199743–53-27) The newly differentiated vernacular, from which Assamese eventually emerged, is evident in the [Prakritisms](/wiki/Kamarupi_Prakrit#Characteristics "Kamarupi Prakrit") present in the Sanskrit of the [Kamarupa inscriptions](/wiki/Kamarupa_inscriptions "Kamarupa inscriptions").[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMedhi198867–63-29)

### Magadhan and Gauda-Kamarupa stages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Magadhan and Gauda-Kamarupa stages")]

The earliest forms of Assamese in literature are found in the 9th-century Buddhist verses called [Charyapada](/wiki/Charyapada "Charyapada")[[29]](#cite_note-30) the language of which bear [affinities with Assamese](/wiki/Charyapada#Affinities_with_Assamese "Charyapada") (as well as Bengali, Maithili and Odia) and which belongs to a period when the Prakrit was at the cusp of differentiating into regional languages.[[30]](#cite_note-31) The spirit and expressiveness of the *Charyadas* are today found in the folk songs called *Deh-Bicarar Git*.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

In the 12th-14th century works of Ramai Pandit (*Sunya Puran*), Boru Chandidas (*Krishna Kirtan*), Sukur Mamud (*Gopichandrar Gan*), Durlabha Mallik (*Gobindachandrar Git*) and Bhavani Das (*Mainamatir Gan*)[[32]](#cite_note-33) Assamese grammatical peculiarities coexist with features from [Bengali language](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language").[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35) Though the Gauda-Kamarupa stage is generally accepted and partially supported by recent linguistic research, it has not been fully reconstructed.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

### Early Assamese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early Assamese")]

See also: [Early Assamese](/wiki/Early_Assamese "Early Assamese") and [Assamese literature § Shankari literature (1490 1700 AD)](/wiki/Assamese_literature#Shankari_literature_(1490_1700_AD) "Assamese literature")

A distinctly Assamese literary form appeared first in the 13th-century in the courts of the [Kamata kingdom](/wiki/Kamata_kingdom "Kamata kingdom") when Hema Sarasvati composed the poem *Prahlāda Carita*.[[36]](#cite_note-37) In the 14th-century, [Madhava Kandali](/wiki/Madhava_Kandali "Madhava Kandali") translated the [Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana") into Assamese ([Saptakanda Ramayana](/wiki/Saptakanda_Ramayana "Saptakanda Ramayana")) in the court of [Mahamanikya](/wiki/Mahamanikya "Mahamanikya"), a [Kachari](/wiki/Bodo-Kachari_peoples "Bodo-Kachari peoples") king from central Assam. Though the Assamese idiom in these works is fully individualised, some archaic forms and conjunctive particles too are found.[[37]](#cite_note-harvcol|Goswami|2003|p=434-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) This period corresponds to the common stage of [proto-Kamta](/wiki/KRNB_lects#Proto_Kamta "KRNB lects") and early Assamese.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

The emergence of [Sankardev](/wiki/Sankardev "Sankardev")'s [Ekasarana Dharma](/wiki/Ekasarana_Dharma "Ekasarana Dharma") in the 15th century triggered a [revival in language and literature](/wiki/Assamese_literature#Shankari_literature_(1490-1700_AD) "Assamese literature").[[40]](#cite_note-41) Sankardev produced many translated works and created new literary forms—*[Borgeets](/wiki/Borgeet "Borgeet")* (songs), *[Ankia Naat](/wiki/Ankia_Naat "Ankia Naat")* (one-act plays)—infusing them with [Brajavali](/wiki/Brajavali "Brajavali") idioms; and these were sustained by his followers [Madhavdev](/wiki/Madhavdev "Madhavdev") and others in the 15th and subsequent centuries. In these writings the 13th/14th-century archaic forms are no longer found. Sankardev pioneered a prose-style of writing in the *Ankia Naat*. This was further developed by [Bhattadeva](/wiki/Bhattadeva "Bhattadeva") who translated the [Bhagavata Purana](/wiki/Bhagavata_Purana "Bhagavata Purana") and [Bhagavad Gita](/wiki/Bhagavad_Gita "Bhagavad Gita") into Assamese prose. Bhattadev's prose was classical and restrained, with a high usage of Sanskrit forms and expressions in an Assamese syntax; and though subsequent authors tried to follow this style, it soon fell into disuse.[[37]](#cite_note-harvcol|Goswami|2003|p=434-38) In this writing the first person future tense ending *-m* (*korim*: "will do"; *kham*: "will eat") is seen for the first time.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

### Middle Assamese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Middle Assamese")]

The language moved to the court of the [Ahom kingdom](/wiki/Ahom_kingdom "Ahom kingdom") in the seventeenth century,[[13]](#cite_note-ahom-court-language-14) where it became the state language. In parallel, the proselytising [Ekasarana dharma](/wiki/Ekasarana_dharma "Ekasarana dharma") converted many Bodo-Kachari peoples and there emerged many new Assamese speakers who were speakers of Tibeto-Burman languages. This period saw the emergence of different styles of secular prose in medicine, astrology, arithmetic, dance, music, besides religious biographies and the archaic prose of magical charms.[[37]](#cite_note-harvcol|Goswami|2003|p=434-38)

Most importantly this was also when Assamese developed a standardised prose in the [Buranjis](/wiki/Buranji "Buranji")—documents related to the Ahom state dealing with diplomatic writings, administrative records and general history.[[37]](#cite_note-harvcol|Goswami|2003|p=434-38) The language of the Buranjis is nearly modern with some minor differences in grammar and with a pre-modern orthography. The Assamese plural suffixes (*-bor*, *-hat*) and the conjunctive participles (*-gai*: *dharile-gai*; *-hi*: *pale-hi*, *baril-hi*) become well established.[[42]](#cite_note-harvcol|Kakati|1953|p=6-43) The Buranjis, dealing with statecraft, was also the vehicle by which Arabic and Persian elements crept into the language in abundance.[[37]](#cite_note-harvcol|Goswami|2003|p=434-38) Due to the influence of the Ahom state the speech in eastern Assam took a homogeneous and standard form.[[43]](#cite_note-44) The general [schwa deletion](/wiki/Schwa_deletion_in_Indo-Aryan_languages#Assamese "Schwa deletion in Indo-Aryan languages") that occurs in the final position of words came into use in this period.

### Modern Assamese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern Assamese")]

The modern period of Assamese begins with printing—the publication of the Assamese Bible in 1813 from the [Serampore Mission Press](/wiki/Serampore_Mission_Press "Serampore Mission Press"). But after the British [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") (EIC) [removed the Burmese](/wiki/Treaty_of_Yandaboo "Treaty of Yandaboo") in 1826 and took complete administrative control of Assam in 1836, it filled administrative positions with people from Bengal, and introduced [Bengali language](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") in its offices, schools and courts.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The EIC had earlier promoted the development of Bengali to replace Persian, the language of administration in Mughal India,[[45]](#cite_note-46) and maintained that Assamese was a dialect of Bengali.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

Amidst this loss of status, the [American Baptist Mission](/wiki/American_Baptist_Mission "American Baptist Mission") (ABM) established a press in Sibsagar in 1846 leading to publications of an Assamese periodical (*[Orunodoi](/wiki/Orunodoi "Orunodoi")*), the first Assamese grammar by [Nathan Brown](/wiki/Nathan_Brown_(missionary) "Nathan Brown (missionary)") (1846), and the first Assamese-English dictionary by [Miles Bronson](/wiki/Miles_Bronson "Miles Bronson") (1863).[[42]](#cite_note-harvcol|Kakati|1953|p=6-43) The ABM argued strongly with the EIC officials in an intense debate in the 1850s to reinstate Assamese.[[47]](#cite_note-48) Among the local personalities, [Anandaram Dhekial Phukan](/wiki/Anandaram_Dhekial_Phukan "Anandaram Dhekial Phukan") drew up an extensive catalogue of medieval Assamese literature (among other works) and pioneered the effort among the natives to reinstate Assamese in Assam.[[48]](#cite_note-49) Though this effort was not immediately successful the administration eventually declared Assamese the official vernacular in 1873 on the eve of Assam becoming a [Chief Commissioner's Province](/wiki/Colonial_Assam#Chief_Commissioner's_Province_(1874–1905) "Colonial Assam") in 1874.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

#### Standardisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Standardisation")]

In the extant medieval Assamese manuscripts the orthography was not uniform. The ABM had evolved a [phonemic orthography](/wiki/Phonemic_orthography "Phonemic orthography") based on a contracted set of characters.[[50]](#cite_note-51) Working independently [Hemchandra Barua](/wiki/Hemchandra_Barua "Hemchandra Barua") provided an etymological orthography and his etymological dictionary, *[Hemkosh](/wiki/Hemkosh "Hemkosh")*, was published posthumously. He also provided a Sanskritised approach to the language in his *Asamiya Bhaxar Byakaran* ("Grammar of the Assamese Language") (1859, 1873).[[51]](#cite_note-52) Barua's approach was adopted by the *[Asamiya Bhasa Unnati Sadhini Sabha](/wiki/Asamiya_Bhasa_Unnati_Sadhini_Sabha "Asamiya Bhasa Unnati Sadhini Sabha")* (1888, "Assamese Language Development Society") that emerged in [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") among Assamese students led by [Lakshminath Bezbaroa](/wiki/Lakshminath_Bezbaroa "Lakshminath Bezbaroa"). The *Society* published a periodical *[Jonaki](/wiki/Jonaki_(magazine) "Jonaki (magazine)")* and the period of its publication, *Jonaki era*, saw spirited negotiations on language standardisation.[[52]](#cite_note-53) What emerged at the end of those negotiations was a standard close to the language of the Buranjis with the Sanskritised orthography of Hemchandra Barua.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

As the political and commercial center moved to [Guwahati](/wiki/Guwahati "Guwahati") in the mid-twentieth century, of which [Dispur](/wiki/Dispur "Dispur") the capital of Assam is a suburb and which is situated at the border between the western and central dialect speaking regions, standard Assamese used in media and communications today is a neutral blend of the eastern variety without its distinctive features.[[54]](#cite_note-55) This core is further embellished with [Goalpariya](/wiki/Goalpariya_dialects "Goalpariya dialects") and [Kamrupi](/wiki/Kamrupi_dialects "Kamrupi dialects") idioms and forms.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

## Geographical distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geographical distribution")]

Assamese is native to [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam"). It is also spoken in states of [Arunachal Pradesh](/wiki/Arunachal_Pradesh "Arunachal Pradesh"), [Meghalaya](/wiki/Meghalaya "Meghalaya") and [Nagaland](/wiki/Nagaland "Nagaland"). The [Bengali-Assamese script](/wiki/Bengali%E2%80%93Assamese_script "Bengali–Assamese script") can be found in of present-day [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma"). The [Pashupatinath Temple](/wiki/Pashupatinath_Temple "Pashupatinath Temple") in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") also has inscriptions in Bengali-Assamese script showing its influence in the past.

There is a significant Assamese-speaking diaspora worldwide.[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

### Official status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Official status")]

Assamese is the [official](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") language of Assam, and one of the 22 [official languages](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India") recognised by the [Republic of India](/wiki/India "India"). The Assam Secretariat functions in Assamese.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

### Consonant clusters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Consonant clusters")]

Main article: [Assamese consonant clusters](/wiki/Assamese_consonant_clusters "Assamese consonant clusters")

### Alveolar stops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Alveolar stops")]

The Assamese [phoneme](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") inventory is unique among [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") as it lacks both postalveolar affricates and fricatives and also a dental-retroflex distinction among the [coronal](/wiki/Coronal_consonant "Coronal consonant") stops.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Historically, the [dental](/wiki/Dental_consonant "Dental consonant") and [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex_consonant "Retroflex consonant") series merged into [alveolar stops](/wiki/Alveolar_plosive "Alveolar plosive"). This makes Assamese resemble non-Indic languages of [Northeast India](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India") (such as [Austroasiatic](/wiki/Austroasiatic_languages "Austroasiatic languages") and [Sino-Tibetan languages](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_languages "Sino-Tibetan languages")).[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoral199745-66) The only other language to have fronted retroflex stops into alveolars is the closely related group of eastern [dialects](/wiki/Dialects "Dialects") of [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") (although a contrast with dental stops remains in those dialects). /[r](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_trill "Voiced alveolar trill")/ is normally realised as [[ɹ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_approximant "Voiced alveolar approximant")] or [[ɻ](/wiki/Voiced_retroflex_approximant "Voiced retroflex approximant")].

### Voiceless velar fricative

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Voiceless velar fricative")]

Assamese is unusual among Eastern Indo-Aryan languages for the presence of /x/ (realised as [[x](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_fricative "Voiceless velar fricative")] or [[χ](/wiki/Voiceless_uvular_fricative "Voiceless uvular fricative")], depending on the speaker and speech register), due historically to the [lenition](/wiki/Lenition "Lenition") of [Middle Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Middle_Indo-Aryan_languages "Middle Indo-Aryan languages") sibilants to /x/ (initially) and /h/ (non-initially).[[66]](#cite_note-67) The use of the [voiceless velar fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_fricative "Voiceless velar fricative") is heavy in the eastern Assamese dialects and decreases progressively to the west from [Kamrupi](/wiki/Kamrupi_dialects "Kamrupi dialects")[[67]](#cite_note-68) to eastern [Goalparia](/wiki/Goalpariya_dialects "Goalpariya dialects"), and disappears completely in western Goalpariya.[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70) The change of /s/ to /h/ and then to /x/ has been attributed to Tibeto-Burman influence by [Suniti Kumar Chatterjee](/wiki/Suniti_Kumar_Chatterji "Suniti Kumar Chatterji").[[70]](#cite_note-71)

In some cases, /kʰ/ can even merge with /χ/ or /x/ (*akhɔr* → *axɔr* "a letter (of an alphabet)").[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMahanta2012219-72)

### Velar nasal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Velar nasal")]

In contrast to other [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"), Assamese, as well as [Odia](/wiki/Odia_language "Odia language") and [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), uses the [velar nasal](/wiki/Velar_nasal "Velar nasal") /ŋ/ (the [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") *ng* in *sing*) extensively. While in many languages, the velar nasal is commonly restricted to preceding [velar](/wiki/Velar_consonant "Velar consonant") sounds, in Assamese it can occur intervocalically.[[72]](#cite_note-iitg2-73) This is another feature it shares with other languages of [Northeast India](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India"), though in Assamese the velar nasal never occurs word-initially.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoral199746-74)

### Vowel inventory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Vowel inventory")]

[Eastern Indic languages](/wiki/Eastern_Indic_languages "Eastern Indic languages") like Assamese, Bengali, [Sylheti](/wiki/Sylheti_language "Sylheti language"), and Odia do not have a [vowel length](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length") distinction, but have a wide set of [back rounded vowels](/wiki/Roundedness "Roundedness"). In the case of Assamese, there are four back rounded vowels that contrast phonemically, as demonstrated by the minimal set: কলা *kola* [kɔla] ('deaf'), ক'লা *kóla* [kola] ('black'), কোলা *kwla* [kʊla] ('lap'), and কুলা *kula* [kula] ('winnowing fan'). The [near-close near-back rounded vowel](/wiki/Near-close_near-back_rounded_vowel "Near-close near-back rounded vowel") /ʊ/ is unique in this branch of the language family. However, in lower Assam, ও is pronounced the same as অ' (ó): compare কোলা *kwla* [kóla] and মোৰ *mwr* [mór].

According to linguist Shakuntala Mahanta, phonemic [nasals vowel](/wiki/Nasal_vowel "Nasal vowel") /ã/ and /ʊ̃/ are present in Assamese, contrasting with /a/ and /ʊ/. They are the only nasal vowel phonemes in the language.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMahanta2012-75)

### Vowel harmony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Vowel harmony")]

Assamese has [vowel harmony](/wiki/Vowel_harmony "Vowel harmony"). The vowels [i] and [u] cause the vowels [ɛ], [ɔ] and [ʊ] in a preceding syllable to change to [e], [o] and [u] respectively. Assamese is one of the few languages spoken in India which exhibit systematic of vowel harmony.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)

### Schwa deletion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Schwa deletion")]

The [inherent vowel](/wiki/Inherent_vowel "Inherent vowel") in standard Assamese, /ɔ/, undergoes [deletion](/wiki/Elision "Elision") in certain environments; this is known as [schwa deletion](/wiki/Schwa_deletion_in_Indo-Aryan_languages "Schwa deletion in Indo-Aryan languages").[[b]](#cite_note-78) This means that, even though the orthography indicates the presence of an [ɔ], the vowel may not be pronounced. In the modern standard, /ɔ/ is generally deleted at the end of a word, except when following /w/ and when following the sequences /ij/ or /uj/.[[77]](#cite_note-79)[[78]](#cite_note-80) It is retained at the end of honorific words.[[79]](#cite_note-:2-81) /ɔ/ is deleted word-internally when it is followed by another vowel and at morphological boundaries.[[79]](#cite_note-:2-81) The rule for deleting the final /ɔ/ was not followed in [Early Assamese](/wiki/Early_Assamese "Early Assamese").

## Writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Writing system")]

Main article: [Assamese alphabet](/wiki/Assamese_alphabet "Assamese alphabet")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Bengali_%E0%A7%B0.svg/250px-Bengali_%E0%A7%B0.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bengali_%E0%A7%B0.svg)

**[Rô](/wiki/Assamese_r%C3%B4 "Assamese rô")**  
One of the [consonants](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") of **[Assamese script](/wiki/Assamese_alphabet "Assamese alphabet")**

Modern Assamese uses the [Assamese](/wiki/Assamese_alphabet "Assamese alphabet") alphabet. In medieval times, the script came in three varieties: *Bamuniya*, *Garhgaya*, and *Kaitheli/Lakhari*, which developed from the [Kamarupi script](/wiki/Kamarupi_script "Kamarupi script"). It very closely resembles the [Mithilakshar](/wiki/Mithilakshar "Mithilakshar") script of the [Maithili language](/wiki/Maithili_language "Maithili language"), as well as the [Bengali alphabet](/wiki/Bengali_alphabet "Bengali alphabet").[[5]](#cite_note-Bora_1981_5,_53-5) There is a strong literary tradition from early times. Examples can be seen in edicts, land grants and copper plates of medieval kings. Assam had its own manuscript writing system on the bark of the *[saanchi](/wiki/Aquilaria_malaccensis "Aquilaria malaccensis")* tree in which religious texts and chronicles were written, as opposed to the pan-Indian system of [Palm leaf manuscript](/wiki/Palm_leaf_manuscript "Palm leaf manuscript") writing. The present-day spellings in Assamese are not necessarily phonetic. *[Hemkosh](/wiki/Hemkosh "Hemkosh")* (হেমকোষ [ɦɛmkʊx]), the second Assamese dictionary, introduced spellings based on [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), which are now the standard.

Assamese has also historically been written using the [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script") by [Assamese Muslims](/wiki/Assamese_Muslims "Assamese Muslims"). One example is *Tariqul Haq Fi Bayane Nurul Haq* by [Zulqad Ali](/w/index.php?title=Zulqad_Ali&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zulqad Ali (page does not exist)") (1796–1891) of [Sivasagar](/wiki/Sivasagar "Sivasagar"), which is one of the oldest works in modern Assamese prose.[[80]](#cite_note-hanif-82)

In the early 1970s, it was agreed upon that the Roman script was to be the standard writing system for [Nagamese Creole](/wiki/Nagamese_Creole "Nagamese Creole").[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) In January 2020, the Assam government announced that Assamese would be a mandatory language for government job eligibility.[[81]](#cite_note-83)

The pot is floating on the river, in such a time a washerman who was washing clothes on the bank of the river saw the pot and wanting to see what's inside, swam towards it, opened the lid and saw that a newly born baby girl was put there and floated by someone. The washerman wanted to adopt the girl, but when he was bringing it towards the shallow water, holding the pot, swimming slowly after having removed the lid, suddenly a kite snatched the girl from the pot and took her away. The kite had her nest on the top of a peepul tree. She put the girl on that nest. Seeing the beautiful little girl, the kite felt affection. Therefore she decided to raise the girl.

## Morphology and grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Morphology and grammar")]

Main article: [Assamese grammar](/wiki/Assamese_grammar "Assamese grammar")

The Assamese language has the following characteristic morphological features:[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKommaluriSubramanianSagar_K2005-84)

* Gender and number are not grammatically marked.
* There is a lexical distinction of gender in the third person pronoun.
* Transitive verbs are distinguished from intransitive.
* The agentive case is overtly marked as distinct from the accusative.
* Kinship nouns are inflected for personal pronominal possession.
* Adverbs can be derived from the verb roots.
* A passive construction may be employed idiomatically.

### Negation process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Negation process")]

Verbs in Assamese are negated by adding /n/ before the verb, with /n/ picking up the initial vowel of the verb. For example:[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoral199747-85)

* /na laɡɛ/ 'do(es) not want' (1st, 2nd and 3rd persons)
* /ni likʰʊ̃/ 'will not write' (1st person)
* /nukutʊ̃/ 'will not nibble' (1st person)
* /nɛlɛkʰɛ/ 'does not count' (3rd person)
* /nɔkɔɹɔ/ 'do not do' (2nd person)

### Classifiers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Classifiers")]

Assamese has a large collection of [classifiers](/wiki/Classifier_(linguistics) "Classifier (linguistics)"), which are used extensively for different kinds of objects, acquired from the [Sino-Tibetan languages](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_languages "Sino-Tibetan languages").[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoral199749–51-86) A few examples of the most extensive and elaborate use of classifiers are given below:

* "***zɔn***" is used to signify a person, male with some amount of respect
  + E.g., manuh-*zɔn* – "the man"
* "***zɔni***" (female) is used after a noun or pronoun to indicate human beings
  + E.g., manuh-*zɔni* – "the woman"
* "***zɔni***" is also used to express the non-human feminine
  + E.g., sɔɹai *zɔni* – "the bird", pɔɹuwa-*zɔni* – "the ant"
* "***zɔna***" and "***gɔɹaki***" are used to express high respect for both man and woman
  + E.g., kɔbi-*zɔna* – "the poet", gʊxaɪ-*zɔna* – "the goddess", rastrapati-*gɔɹaki* – "the president", tiɹʊta-*gɔɹaki* – "the woman"
* "***tʊ***" has three forms: ***tʊ***, ***ta***, ***ti***
  + (a) tʊ: is used to specify something, although the case of someone, e.g., loɹa-*tʊ* – "the particular boy", is impolite
  + (b) ta: is used only after numerals, e.g., ɛ*ta*, du*ta*, tini*ta* – "one, two, three"
  + (c) ti: is the diminutive form, e.g., kesua-*ti* – "the infant, besides expressing more affection or attachment to
* "***kɔsa***", "***mɔtʰa***" and "***taɹ***" are used for things in bunches
  + E.g., sabi-*kɔsa* – "the bunch of key", saul-*mɔtʰa* – "a handful of rice", suli-*taɹi* or suli *kɔsa* – "the bunch of hair"
* ***dal***, ***dali***, are used after nouns to indicate something long but round and solid
  + E.g., bãʱ-*dal* – "the bamboo", katʰ-*dal* – "the piece of wood", bãʱ-*dali* – "the piece of bamboo"

In Assamese, classifiers are generally used in the *numeral + classifier + noun* (e.g. /ezɔn manuh/ ejon manuh 'one man') or the *noun + numeral + classifier* (e.g. /manuh ezɔn/ manuh ejon 'one man') forms.

### Nominalization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Nominalization")]

Most verbs can be converted into nouns by the addition of the suffix /ɔn/. For example, /kʰa/ ('to eat') can be converted to /kʰaɔn/ khaon ('good eating').[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoral199748-87)

The negative forms are *n + 1st vowel of the verb + the verb*. Example: Moi porhw, Moi noporhw (I read, I do not read); Tumi khelila, Tumi nekhelila (You played, You didn't play). For verbs that start with a vowel, just the *n-* is added, without vowel lengthening. In some dialects if the 1st vowel is *a* in a verb that starts with consonant, *ne* is used, like, *Moi nakhaw* (I don't eat) is *Moi nekhaü*. In past continuous the negative form is *-i thoka nasil-*. In future continuous it's *-i na(/e)thaki-*. In present continuous and present perfect, just *-i thoka nai* and *-a nai'* respectively are used for all personal pronouns. Sometimes for plural pronouns, the *-hok* suffix is used, like *korwhok* (we do), *ahilahok* (you guys came).Content

### Regional dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Regional dialects")]

The language has quite a few regional variations. Banikanta Kakati identified two broad [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") which he named (1) Eastern and (2) Western dialects,[[3]](#cite_note-Kakati_1941_16-3) of which the eastern dialect is homogeneous, and prevalent to the east of Guwahati, and the western dialect is heterogeneous. However, recent linguistic studies have identified four dialect groups and one dialect isolate listed below from east to west to south:[[61]](#cite_note-iitg-62)[[86]](#cite_note-88)

* **Eastern group** in and around the undivided Sivasagar district ([Golaghat](/wiki/Golaghat_district "Golaghat district"), [Jorhat](/wiki/Jorhat_district "Jorhat district"), [Majuli](/wiki/Majuli_district "Majuli district"), [Charaideo](/wiki/Charaideo_district "Charaideo district") and [Sivasagar](/wiki/Sivasagar_district "Sivasagar district")) and the former undivided Lakhimpur district ([Dibrugarh](/wiki/Dibrugarh_district "Dibrugarh district"), [Tinsukia](/wiki/Tinsukia_district "Tinsukia district"), [Lakhimpur](/wiki/Lakhimpur_district "Lakhimpur district") and [Dhemaji](/wiki/Dhemaji_district "Dhemaji district"). Standard Assamese is based on the Eastern group.
* **Central group** spoken in [Nagaon](/wiki/Nagaon_district "Nagaon district"), [Sonitpur](/wiki/Sonitpur_district "Sonitpur district"), [Morigaon](/wiki/Morigaon_district "Morigaon district") districts and adjoining areas
* **[Kamrupi](/wiki/Kamrupi_dialect "Kamrupi dialect") group** in the [Kamrup region](/wiki/Kamrup_region "Kamrup region"): ([Barpetia](/wiki/Barpetia_dialect "Barpetia dialect"), [Nalbariya](/wiki/Nalbariya_dialect "Nalbariya dialect"), Palasbaria)
* **[Goalpariya](/wiki/Goalpariya_dialect "Goalpariya dialect") group** in the [Goalpara region](/wiki/Goalpara_region "Goalpara region"): (Ghulliya, Jharuwa, Caruwa)
* **[Cachar dialect](/wiki/Dehan_dialect "Dehan dialect")** (Dehan) in [Cachar district](/wiki/Cachar_district "Cachar district") of [Barak valley](/wiki/Barak_valley "Barak valley")

### Non-regional dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Non-regional dialects")]

Assamese does not have many caste- or occupation-based dialects.[[87]](#cite_note-89) In the nineteenth century, the Eastern dialect became the standard dialect because it witnessed more literary activity and it was more uniform from east of Guwahati to Sadiya,[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKakati194114-16-90) whereas the western dialects were more heterogeneous.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoswami2003436-91) Since the nineteenth century, the center of literary activity (as well as of politics and commerce) has shifted to Guwahati; as a result, the standard dialect has evolved considerably away from the largely rural Eastern dialects and has become more urban and acquired western dialectal elements.[[90]](#cite_note-Dutta2003p106-92) Most literary activity takes place in this dialect, and is often called the *likhito-bhaxa*, though regional dialects are often used in novels and other creative works.

In addition to the regional variants, sub-regional, community-based dialects are also prevalent, namely:

* **Standard dialect** influenced by surrounding centers.
* ***Bhakatiya* dialect** highly polite, a sattra-based dialect with a different set of nominals, pronominals, and verbal forms, as well as a preference for euphemism; indirect and passive expressions.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoswami2003439–440-93) Some of these features are used in the standard dialect on very formal occasions.
* The fisherman community has a dialect that is used in the central and eastern region.
* The astrologer community of Darrang district has a dialect called *thar* that is coded and secretive. The *ratikhowa* and *bhitarpanthiya* secretive cult-based Vaisnava groups too have their own dialects.[[92]](#cite_note-Dutta2003p107-94)
* The Muslim community have their own dialectal preference, with their own kinship, custom, and religious terms, with those in east Assam having distinct phonetic features.[[90]](#cite_note-Dutta2003p106-92)
* The urban adolescent and youth communities (for example, Guwahati) have exotic, hybrid and local slangs.[[90]](#cite_note-Dutta2003p106-92)
* Ethnic speech communities that use Assamese as a second language, often use dialects that are influenced heavily by the pronunciation, intonation, stress, vocabulary and syntax of their respective first languages (*Mising Eastern Assamese*, *Bodo Central Kamrupi*, *Rabha Eastern Goalpariya* etc.).[[92]](#cite_note-Dutta2003p107-94) Two independent pidgins/creoles, associated with the Assamese language, are [Nagamese](/wiki/Nagamese "Nagamese") (used by [Naga](/wiki/Naga_people "Naga people") groups) and [Nefamese](/wiki/Nefamese "Nefamese") (used in Arunachal Pradesh).[[93]](#cite_note-95)

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Assamese_language&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Literature")]

Main article: [Assamese literature](/wiki/Assamese_literature "Assamese literature")

There is a growing and strong body of literature in this language.
The first characteristics of this language are seen in the [Charyapadas](/wiki/Charyapada "Charyapada") composed in between the eighth and twelfth centuries. The first examples emerged in writings of court poets in the fourteenth century, the finest example of which is Madhav Kandali's [Saptakanda Ramayana](/wiki/Saptakanda_Ramayana "Saptakanda Ramayana"). The popular ballad in the form of Ojapali is also regarded as well-crafted. The sixteenth and seventeenth centuries saw a flourishing of [Vaishnavite](/wiki/Vaishnavite "Vaishnavite") literature, leading up to the emergence of modern forms of literature in the late nineteenth century.
